Many American cities are trying to keep their downtowns vibrant.
In Cleveland, some of the grand old office building have been repurposed into hotels and apartment complexes.
And now a gilded-age structure that resembles the Pantheon in Rome has been turned into one of the nation's most unusual shopping experiences.
Here's Mark Urycki from member station WCPN.
In 1908, the Cleveland Trust Bank opened in a new granite neoclassical building in the city's financial district.
Inside is a round lobby surrounded by marble columns and a stained-glass dome looming 85 feet above the main floor.
Last year, architect John Williams was given the assignment of his career - turning that space into a grocery store - yep, a grocery store.
It's incredibly exciting and incredibly terrifying to be responsible for this - one of the most important buildings in the region - and that has been closed to the public for over 25 years.
The grocery chain Heinenâs Fine Foods operates 21 suburban stores in Cleveland and Chicago.
But this is their first downtown store.
Jeff Heinen admits it's a lot like pounding a square peg into a round hole.
The grocery business tends to think in terms of squares and rectangles and not so much in terms of being round.
And so trying to fit the shape of the building - it's been an interesting challenge.
Yeah, the architect would agree with that.
The rotunda is inside a pentagon-shaped room with no two walls the same length.
It's been a bloody nightmare.
It also has two ornate balconies and way too many entrances.
But those obstacles were overcome.
And last week, it opened for business.
(SOUNDBITE OF GROCERY STORE BAKERY)
UNIDENTIFIED WOMAN #1: Mini cannoli please,
UNIDENTIFIED WOMAN #2: OK, what flavor?
I have pistachio, cherry, regular and chocolate.
UNIDENTIFIED WOMAN #1: Pistachio and pink.
UNIDENTIFIED WOMAN #2: OK.
Shoppers can't help but look up and take photos of the stained-glass dome and the 13 murals surrounding it.
Fred Geis and his brother own the building and just renovated an adjacent office tower into a hotel apartment complex.
Geis says it's important for downtown residents to have a place to buy groceries.
There's not a day I walk in the store I don't rub my eyes because I can't believe I own the facility, right?
And then I rub my eyes even more that we've got a real grocery store here.
And now to the Republicans.
This week in Washington, the Republican National Committee approved Florida Senator Mel Martinez as the party's general chairman.
As NPR's Andrea Hsu reports, the title was created just for him.
It's definitely not the most stylish outfit, but it's something that's more relative to tradition.
And it's kind of neat that the jacket didn't change throughout the years.
It's always been blue corduroy, same emblem and everything.
One tradition that's held on, while everything around it changed.
For NPR News, I'm Luke Runyon in Greeley, Colo.
(SOUNDBITE OF MUSIC)
And that story came to us from Harvest Public Media, a reporting collaboration focusing on agriculture and food.
This is NPR News.

In spite of the brutality on display in the videos from ISIS, the terrorists are not having trouble attracting fighters to their cause.
Shadi Hamid is a fellow at the Brookings Institution Center for Middle East Policy.
He's been studying how the so-called Islamic State differs from past Islamist groups.
Earlier this week, he told me that one of the secrets to their success is a cunning use of language designed to evoke deep sympathies from Muslims, like their distinctive use of the term caliphate.
So if we look at other extremist groups, including al-Qaida, they would talk about the caliphate in theory, as an aspiration.
And that's where ISIS is so revolutionary - is they said, we're actually going to establish a real caliphate on actual territory.
So from now on, any time there's an ungoverned or ungovernable space, an extremist group might ask itself, why not establish our own mini caliphate in this piece of territory?

And that's why even if ISIS was defeated entirely tomorrow morning, they would've still succeeded in this way and many other ways because they've set a different precedent for extremist groups going forward.
And ISIS has been very good at recruiting soldiers, even from across borders.
Can you talk about the religious language they use - the particular language they use and how that helps them with recruitment?
So in this sense, ISIS has a built-in advantage because their fighters are willing to die, and they believe that they will be granted direct entry into heaven.
So that kind of ideological power gives ISIS internal cohesion.
It gives it moral.
And that's very useful when they're fighting against, say, the Iraqi army, which totally lacks moral.
And that's why we had a situation in June where ISIS was able to overtake Mosul, Iraq's second-largest city, with about 800 fighters at first.
And the Iraqi army and police forces had about 30,000.
So in this sense, the power of ideology and religious inspiration is a force-multiplier.
You write that ISIS' rise has something to do with Islam, but you struggle with what something is.
How is ISIS different from other groups that seek to establish a state governed by Islamic law?

So with the Arab Spring, we had mainstream Islamist groups, like the Muslim Brotherhood, who tried to work within the democratic process to promote their vision of a more Islamic state, but in a gradual, slow, plodding way.

So what - ISIS is coming in, and they're saying that the Muslim Brotherhood model of working within the political process is an utter failure and that the only way to achieve an Islamic State is by imposing through brute force.

And this, I think, draws on one of the unfortunate lessons of the post-Arab Spring period - is that violence actually does work sometimes, whether it's from autocratic regimes or extremist groups, like ISIS.
And ISIS is also distinct that - in that, unlike al-Qaida, they take governance more seriously.
They're able to run local administrations, provide for some degree of law and order, provide social services.
And that's really appealing in the Iraqi and Syrian contexts, where there's really a power vacuum and there's lack of legitimate government.
Shadi Hamid is a fellow at the Brookings Institution Center for Middle East Policy.
His new book is ""Temptations Of Power: Islamists And The Liberal Democracy In A New Middle East."" Shadi, thanks very much.
Thanks for having me.
Health benefits for employees of larger companies are typically some of the most generous in the nation.
Most of those workers won't be using the exchanges set up by the Affordable Care Act, but they will help pay for them.
Sarah Jane Tribble of member station WCPN in Cleveland reports on a new fee that affects many large employers.
The world of health care, like any, is full of haves and have-nots.
It's not hard to the haves at Sherwin-Williams' corporate headquarters in downtown Cleveland where about 2,500 employees have access to an in-house health and wellness center.
The company encourages them to take a break from their work and go to an exercise class, hop on an elliptical trainer or go for a run on the treadmill.
It's part of Sherwin-Williams investment in employees for the long term.
Like other self-insured employers, it has a strong incentive to keep workers healthy.
That's because any healthcare claims by employees are paid directly by the company.
The corporation is betting it will be better off paying those costs rather than paying a certain price per month to an insurance company.
The key is to have healthy, engaged, productive, present employees.
That's Martha Lanning, the company's director of health and wellness plans.
Sherwin-Williams and its employees are not likely to use the individual marketplace created by Obamacare, but they will help pay for it.
Lanning says companies like Sherwin-Williams must devote staff members to sorting through the legal and administrative issues connected to the Affordable Care Act.
These efforts cost money and, she says...
We also have to pay this transitional reinsurance fee that totals for us, with some other aspects of the Affordable Care Act, about $4 million in 2014.
The government collects $63 from large employers for each employee and for each dependent it insures.
That money goes into a fund for health insurance companies.
The fund reimburses insurance companies for some extra costs in providing coverage to high-risk people on the Obamacare exchanges.
You might think of it as a subsidy from big business for making insurance coverage available to everyone without regard to preexisting conditions.
Mark Hall, a professor of law and public health at Wake Forest University, says it's a sensible solution to a problem, he says, is temporary.
The great feature of the Affordable Care Act is it now makes everyone qualify for coverage, but that means the first people to sign up or to show up are going to be the folks who need it the most.
So we expect, particularly at the outset, that there is going to be a heavy load on the insurance pool of high-cost people.
